subject: obstetrics-gynecology
title: Vaginal bleeding
preambleMarkdown:
# Vaginal bleeding

> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Vaginal bleeding is an obstetric, gynecologic, or reproductive health topic in obstetrics gynecology.
- This article focuses on pregnancy status, reproductive anatomy, bleeding, infection, fertility, fetal risk, and obstetric urgency.
- The practical clinical priority is maternal stabilization, reproductive anatomy, and fetal or fertility implications.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Vaginal bleeding to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Vaginal bleeding is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is maternal stabilization, reproductive anatomy, and fetal or fertility implications.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pregnant, postpartum, reproductive-age nonpregnant, perimenopausal, or postmenopausal context.
- Bleeding, pain, infection, hypertensive, endocrine, fertility, contraception, malignancy, or procedural presentation.
- First, second, third trimester, intrapartum, or postpartum timing when pregnancy is present.
- Stable outpatient condition versus emergency requiring obstetric or gynecologic intervention.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Vaginal bleeding depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In obstetrics gynecology, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Vaginal bleeding.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Vaginal bleeding disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Vaginal bleeding should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Vaginal bleeding first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Vaginal bleeding, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Key symptoms include bleeding, pelvic or abdominal pain, discharge, fever, contractions, fetal movement change, urinary symptoms, and hemodynamic instability.
- Pregnancy status and gestational age change the differential diagnosis and the safest tests.
- Postpartum patients remain at risk for hemorrhage, infection, thromboembolism, hypertensive disease, and mood disorders.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Vaginal bleeding, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused obstetrics gynecology examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Vaginal bleeding presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Vaginal bleeding with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Vaginal bleeding is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Vaginal bleeding should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize the mother first, then assess fetal status when gestational age makes that relevant.
- Use pregnancy-safe diagnostics and medications when possible, but do not withhold urgent maternal care.
- Involve obstetrics or gynecology early for ectopic concern, heavy bleeding, sepsis, fetal distress, severe hypertension, or operative need.

### Initial Management

- Stabilize the mother first, then assess fetal status when gestational age makes fetal assessment meaningful.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses maternal stabilization, reproductive anatomy, and fetal or fertility implications.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use procedures when they confirm diagnosis, obtain tissue or fluid, provide source control, relieve obstruction, restore alignment, repair anatomy, or treat complications.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Vaginal bleeding, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Vaginal bleeding may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Vaginal bleeding patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Vaginal bleeding, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Vaginal bleeding in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Vaginal bleeding, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Vaginal bleeding does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Vaginal bleeding may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Vaginal bleeding, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Vaginal bleeding as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pregnancy test is mandatory in reproductive-age patients with abdominal or pelvic pain unless pregnancy is impossible.
- Maternal resuscitation is fetal resuscitation.
- Ectopic pregnancy, postpartum hemorrhage, eclampsia, and sepsis are cannot-miss emergencies.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Vaginal bleeding draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

### Source Excerpt: Vaginal Bleeding during Pregnancy

_Source lines: 25446-25756._

```text
III. VAGINAL BLEEDING DURING PREGNANCY
A. First-trimester bleeding
1. Ectopic pregnancy is discussed in section II.

## 2. Abortion/miscarriage

a. Discussion. Approximately 20% of all pregnancies spontaneously termi-
nate, and 80% to 90% of these end as miscarriage during the first trimester
(weeks 1 to 14). Risk factors include previous miscarriage, increased mater-
nal parity, and increased maternal age.

b. Clinical features. Fetal demise usually occurs 2 to 3 weeks prior to the
onset of clinical symptoms. Different types of abortions are described in
Clinical Pearl 14-1.

Quick 	HIT
A FAST scan (focused as-
sessment with sonography
for trauma)done by the ED
physician can quickly make
the diagnosis of free fluid in
the abdomen.

1. Threatened abortion is most common in the ED. The patient complains of vaginal bleeding, which may
be accompanied by abdominal pain. Examination reveals blood in the vaginal vault, a closed internal cervi-
cal os, and an enlarged, tender uterus. Quantitative !-hCG levels may not correlate with the gestational
age according to the last menstrual period.

2. Inevitable abortion presents in the same manner as threatened abortion, except the internal cervical os
is open on examination.

3. Incomplete abortion may yield findings similar to inevitable abortion, except blood or gestational prod-
ucts are noted in the cervical canal and vaginal vault on examination. The internal cervical os is open.

4. Completed abortion is rarely diagnosed in the ED and not without obstetric consultation. The patient
may report a history that would suggest vaginal passage of large amounts of blood and gestational prod-
ucts. Upon examination, the internal cervical os is closed and the uterus is nontender. Diagnosis cannot be
made unless the patient actually miscarries in the ED, allowing identification of an intact gestational sac
and products.

5. Missed abortion occurs when fetal products are retained in utero despite their demise. The patient may
complain of frequent episodic abdominal cramping and vaginal spotting. Examination reveals a closed
internal cervical os. Quantitative !-hCG levels are low for the gestational age, and ultrasound reveals
no fetal cardiac activity. Fetal products may remain for prolonged periods and may need to be surgically
removed.

6. Septic abortion usually begins as an inevitable abortion complicated by intrauterine infection due to pro-
longed os opening, repeated examinations, or instrumentation. The patient is febrile and has an elevated
white blood cell count, with cervical motion and uterine tenderness noted on examination.

PEARL	CLINICAL 	14-1
O B S T E T R I C A N D G Y N E C O L O G I C E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	317

Obstetric and Gynecologic Emergencies
c. Differential diagnoses. Although 15% of first-trimester pregnancies terminate
as miscarriage, ectopic pregnancy must top the differential list until it can be
ruled out by examination, ultrasonography, or surgical findings. Other differ-
ential diagnoses include normal intrauterine pregnancy, molar pregnancy, vul-
vovaginitis, cervicitis, urinary tract infection, PID, and vaginal foreign body.

i. Molar pregnancy (hydatidiform mole) is defined as placental prolifera-
tion without fetal tissues.

a) Pathogenesis. Hydatidiform mole is associated with chromosomal
anomalies producing trophoblastic hyperplasia and vascular, grape-
like chorionic villi. Hydatidiform mole carries the risk of subsequent
choriocarcinoma.

b) Risk factors include maternal age greater than 40 years or less than
20 years.

c) Features include painless vaginal bleeding, most commonly before
20 weeks' gestation. Uterine size is large for gestational age. Rarely,
patients may exhibit symptoms of preeclampsia before they reach the
20-week gestation mark.

d. Evaluation
i. Patient history. The date of the last menstrual period must be identi-
fied. The patient should be questioned about the amount, character,
and time course of the bleeding. A history of trauma, fevers, and associ-
ated pain should be sought.

ii. Laboratory studies. The workup should proceed as for ectopic
pregnancy (see II.D).

iii. Microscopic examination. All expulsed products of conception must be
examined microscopically for the presence of a gestational sac, chori-
onic villi, or fetal material before miscarriage can be diagnosed.

iv. Molar pregnancy. Ultrasound is usually diagnostic, revealing a
"snowstorm" intrauterine pattern and absence of fetal heart activity
(Figure 14-2).

e. Therapy
i. A hemodynamically stable patient with no clinical evidence suggesting
inevitable or incomplete abortion and no clinical or sonographic evidence
supporting ectopic or intrauterine pregnancy has an ectopic pregnancy
until proven otherwise. These patients must undergo repeated quantita-
tive !-hCG levels every 48 hours and sonograms every week until the
pregnancy is located and !-hCG levels dictate viability or demise.

F I G U R E
14-2 	Ultrasound image of molar pregnancy.

318 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Obstetric and Gynecologic Emergencies
ii. Intrauterine abortion may occur spontaneously and requires little
intervention.

a) Patients with clinical evidence of inevitable, incomplete, or missed
abortion may typically undergo expectant management. Medical
management is an option but may require dilatation and curettage
to reduce the risk of retained products, continued bleeding, and
infection. Misoprostol or oxytocin may be used after dilatation and
curettage to induce uterine contraction and reduce bleeding.

b) Patients who are hemodynamically unstable despite an aggressive
resuscitative effort require surgical intervention (e.g., dilatation and
curettage, hysterectomy).

iii. Molar pregnancy. After sonographic documentation, dilatation and cu-
rettage is necessary to ensure complete removal, which reduces the risk
of subsequent choriocarcinoma. Follow-up serial ! -hCG levels should
be obtained.

f. Disposition
i. Hemodynamically stable patients with no evidence of ongoing hemor-
rhage, pelvic bleeding, or intraperitoneal bleeding and no findings sug-
gesting inevitable or incomplete miscarriage may be discharged after
obstetric follow-up has been arranged. These patients should see an
obstetrician within 24 to 48 hours for repeat quantitative !-hCG and
sonography.

ii. Hemodynamically unstable patients are admitted for definitive operative
therapy and observation.

3. Normal intrauterine pregnancy may be associated with vaginal bleeding, espe-
cially during the first trimester. Patients may experience abdominal cramping
with vaginal spotting, but on examination, the cervical os is closed and the
uterus is nontender. This bleeding is not from the cervical os and is typically
from local inflammation, infection, or trauma.

a. Serial quantitative ! -hCG levels should still be trended and increase nor-
mally (i.e., they double every 36 to 48 hours, peaking at 100,000 mIU by 8
to 11 weeks).

b. After 6 weeks' gestation, ultrasound reveals an intrauterine pregnancy with
fetal heart activity.

B. Second- and third-trimester bleeding

## 1. Discussion

a. Placenta previa
i. Pathogenesis. Placenta previa occurs when a portion of the placenta
implants on the lower uterine segment. As the uterus grows or as the
cervix dilates, more or less of the os may be covered by placenta.

ii. Risk factors include increasing maternal age, increasing maternal parity,
and prior uterine scarring from surgery.

b. Placental abruption (abruptio placentae) and subchorionic hemorrhage
occurs when the placenta separates from the decidua basalis of the uterus
prior to stage III of labor. Separation can be partial (subchorionic hemor-
rhage) or complete. Vaginal bleeding may occur, or the hemorrhage may be
concealed under the placenta away from the cervical os.

i. Pathogenesis. Associated with defective placental vasculature
ii. Risk factors include multiparity, increased maternal age, previous
abruption, pregnancy-induced hypertension, a short umbilical cord,
trauma, smoking, and cocaine abuse.

c. Uterine dehiscence and rupture is failure of the myometrial wall to ad-
equately contain the contents of the uterus.

i. Pathogenesis. Typically occurs during labor but also can occur during late
pregnancy. Weakness in myometrium may allow blood and uterine wall
(dehiscence) or all uterine contents (rupture) into the abdominal cavity.

ii. Risk factors include any type of uterine surgery, use of medications to
induce labor, and multiparity.

Quick 	HIT
Patients with clinical or
sonographic data suggesting
pelvic intraperitoneal hemor-
rhage must undergo surgical
exploration.

O B S T E T R I C A N D G Y N E C O L O G I C E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	319

Obstetric and Gynecologic Emergencies
2. Clinical features
a. Placenta previa. Patients present with spontaneous, painless, bright red vag-
inal bleeding, either slight or profuse. Initial bleeding may cease suddenly,
only to recur.

b. Placental abruption or subchorionic hemorrhage
i. Fetal distress or vaginal bleeding are cardinal signs. Sudden abdominal
or pelvic pain is seen in 90% of patients with abruption. Shock is vari-
ably present, and fetal well-being should be monitored.

ii. The uterus may be tender, hypertonic, or enlarged for gestational age
(due to bleeding). Premature labor may be present. Fetal compromise is
variably present.

c. Uterine dehiscence or rupture. Symptoms can initially be mild abdominal
pain but quickly lead to fetal compromise, shock, and death.

3. Differential diagnoses include trauma, blood dyscrasias and cirrhosis, and es-
trogen excess state.

## 4. Evaluation

a. Stabilization. Maternal resuscitation is the highest priority. Once the patient's
airway, breathing, and circulatory status have been stabilized, historical infor-
mation should be obtained. Blood typing and screening (including ABO and Rh
sensitivity), quantitative !-hCG studies, a CBC, and coagulation profile are sent
immediately. Fetal monitoring is established as appropriate for gestational age.

b. Physical examination of patients with second- or third-trimester bleeding
should be performed in consultation with an obstetrician in the operating
room to minimize the potential for hemorrhagic catastrophe, particularly
when uterine rupture or placental abruption is suspected.

c. Laboratory studies
i. Quantitative !-hCG levels are abnormally high (500,000 to
1,000,000 mIU; the normal peak is 100,000 mIU) in molar pregnancy.

ii. Coagulation studies may reveal low fibrinogen, diminished factors V
and VIII, and a low platelet count in patients with placental abruption.

d. Ultrasonography. Uterine ultrasound can make the distinction between
placenta previa and placental abruption. Its use is paramount, especially in
patients with second- or third-trimester bleeding, because complete pelvic
examination is contraindicated in these patients until placenta previa and
placental abruption are ruled out.

i. Placenta previa. Ultrasound is the diagnostic test of choice to document
the location and extent of previa. The placental margin covers part or all
of the os.

ii. Placental abruption. Ultrasonography is not sensitive and should be
used in conjunction with tocometry to measure fetal distress. It may re-
veal retroplacental clots.

## 5. Therapy

a. Rho(D) immune globulin is given to all Rh-negative mothers experiencing
hemorrhage in pregnancy to prevent maternal Rh antibody formation and
risk of hydrops fetalis with any future pregnancy.

b. Hemodynamically stable patients with no evidence of fetal distress. Strict
bed rest and observation with frequent fetal checks until the fetus reaches
maturity are indicated for stable patients with no evidence of fetal distress
but who have ultrasonographic evidence of placenta previa or abruption.

Fetal maturity is documented by dates, sonography, and amniotic phospho-
lipid ratios (see VI.B.1.c.ii).

c. Hemodynamically stable patients with fetal distress. If the fetus is less than
23 weeks, the maternal environment should be optimized with expectant man-
agement. A fetus younger than 23 weeks cannot survive outside of the uterus
and is considered a second-trimester miscarriage. If the fetus is greater than
23 weeks and cannot be stabilized, emergent cesarean section is warranted.

d. Hemodynamically unstable patients with or without evidence of fetal dis-
tress. Aggressive fluid and blood resuscitation, positioning of the mother in
Quick 	HIT
Babies need their mother to
survive in utero, so mater-
nal stabilization is the first
priority.

Quick 	HIT
In a viable fetus, a quick his-
tory and physical can neces-
sitate emergent cesarean
section in cases of uterine
rupture (peritonitis) and
placental abruption (tender,
hard uterus).

320 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Obstetric and Gynecologic Emergencies
the left lateral decubitus position, and emergent delivery by cesarean section
if the fetus is older than 23 weeks are indicated. Oxytocin is administered
after placental delivery to reduce further bleeding.

e. Placenta previa typically is managed by cesarean section when time for deliv-
ery comes, although in cases of mild previa, vaginal delivery is still possible.

f. Uterine rupture or complete placental abruption with or without maternal
instability with cause fetal compromise and necessitates immediate delivery.

6. Disposition. Patients with placenta previa and mild bleeding do not always
get admitted but should have obstetrical consultation. All other patients with
second- or third-trimester bleeding should be admitted for further sonographic
evaluation, fetal maturity studies, maternal-fetal monitoring, bed rest, observa-
tion, delivery, and/or surgery.
```

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.
